title: AI Usage in the U.S. Companies
text:
Motivation
With how much companies are adopting artificial intelligence into its services, it seems like nowadays it’s impossible to escape from it. This is especially noticeable with tech companies, to the point where AI has become a necessity in an expanding tech landscape. It’s almost hard to imagine that life wasn’t always like this — not 20 years, 15, nor even 10 years ago.
This is precisely why we chose this topic for our final project.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

text:
Research Question

What are the AI adoption trends across industries and firms in the U.S.?

title: AI Usage in the U.S. Companies
section: Background: Where Are We Seeing AI Job Cuts in 2026?
text:
Background: Where Are We Seeing AI Job Cuts in 2026?
Throughout the U.S., finding a job is harder than ever. Job seekers, especially entry-level workers and recent/upcoming colleges graduates, are growing increasingly worried as they attempt to find employment in the era of AI. Due to employment-related concerns, some are even considering opting out of the job hunt entirely: 88.4% of graduates from a recent 2026 ZipRecruiter survey reported considering gap years, graduate school, and other non-employment options in lieu of continuing the search for work (Nietzel 2026).
Despite c-suite excitement over the potential of AI, the fears of these job-seekers are not unfounded. Since last October, major companies with operations across the U.S. have announced impending job cuts, with some major companies such as Amazon and HSBC Holdings reporting an estimated 16K and 20K layoffs planned respectively (Reuters 2026). Though extreme cases, these companies are only two of many who are in the process of AI restructuring, at the cost of their human capital.
Understanding the current state of AI in firms, let’s now review what the AI landscape looked like in 2018.







Figure 1: Major job cuts due to AI from the last few months (2025-2026). Hover over the viz to see specific companies & industries.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI Usage in the U.S. Companies
text:
Data
This project analyzes two datasets from the U.S. Census Bureau’s Annual Business Survey (ABS), accessed through the Census Data API.
The first is the ABSTCB (Annual Business Survey: Technology Characteristics of Businesses, 2018), which captures how U.S. employer firms used, produced, and were impacted by various technologies — including artificial intelligence, cloud computing, robotics, specialized software, and specialized equipment. This is the only year for which this technology module is publicly available, making 2018 our reference point for AI adoption patterns.
The second is the ABSCS (Annual Business Survey: Company Summary, 2017–2021), which provides firm-level aggregates including number of firms, employment, annual payroll, and revenue across industries and states over time. This panel dataset allows us to track how industries evolved before and after the 2018 AI snapshot.
At a Glance



0

0

Data Collection, and Acquisition
Both datasets come from the U.S. Census Bureau’s Annual Business Survey (ABS), conducted annually since 2018. The ABS covers all nonfarm employer businesses in the United States and provides nationally representative data on firm characteristics, ownership, innovation, and technology use. We accessed both datasets through the Census Data API using R’s httr2 package and built a reusable function to retrieve data by year, module, and geography. The technology module was collected at the state level for all 50 states and Washington, D.C., while the company summary panel spans 2017–2021.
Our analysis proceeds in five steps. First, we examine recent job cuts in 2026 and late 2025 based on reporting from Reuters. Second, we analyze state-level revenue in 2017 using the ABS panel (as this is the only year with complete revenue data). Third, we identify the highest-revenue industries within each state. Fourth, we study AI adoption in 2018 and track subsequent payroll changes across industries. Finally, we estimate the wage premium associated with AI. To complement this analysis, we use a Random Forest model to predict AI adoption across sectors, evaluating performance using both Gini impurity and permutation importance.

1. Which Industries Report the Highest Wages?
Management, Information, Finance & Insurance, and “Utilities” reported some of the highest average wages in 2018. Employee numbers were also lower in these industries relative to others. The four key industries are distinguished by color. But… what does this have to do with AI?







Figure 2: Management, Information, Finance & Insurance, and Utilities Lead for highest average wages and some of the lowest employment numbers.



2. Who Led the AI Race in 2018?
Management of Companies & Enterprises led AI adoption at 7.7%. Those industries where AI-compatible tasks (forecasting, automation, analysis) were most embedded in daily operations. Knowledge-intensive sectors moved first.







Figure 3: Management led the AI race in 2018 with an AI implementation rate of 7.7%.



3. Did AI Adoption Translate to Payroll Growth?
Tracking payroll from 2017 to 2021, the four highest AI-adopting industries consistently outpaced the rest. The grey lines of lower-adoption sectors trail below, suggesting a structural divergence — not a short-term blip.







Figure 4: The top four industries by AI adoption maintained steady payroll growth, whereas the bottom four displayed volatility, suggesting a potential link between AI integration and workforce stability.



4. The Revenue Landscape Before AI (2017)
But did AI adoption simply follow the money? The 2017 revenue snapshot reveals a disconnection: Retail Trade, Manufacturing, and Healthcare, the three biggest industries by revenue, were mid-to-low AI adopters. Management of Companies, the AI leader, barely registers here. Wealth and early adoption diverged.






Note



FUN FACT: The US Census Bureau also reported this exact figure as in our visualization below.









Figure 5: Wholesale Trade topped all industries in 2017, generating $8.6 trillion USD in revenue.



5. The AI Wage Gap
Setting revenue aside, the wage difference between AI-adopting and non-adopting firms is consistent. Across nearly every sector, workers at AI firms earned more, not by a little, but by thousands of dollars per year. Hover over any industry to see both figures precisely.







Figure 6: Across nearly all industries in 2018, workers at AI-adopting firms earned higher average annual payroll per employee than their counterparts at non-adopting firms.



6. The AI Wage Premium by Industry
Expressing the gap as a percentage premium reveals a striking inversion: the industries where AI adoption was lowest in 2018 often show the highest wage premium. Food Services leads at ~86%, Healthcare follows at ~68%, both low-adoption sectors. In Finance & Insurance, a top adopter, the premium has already compressed to ~24%. Early movers still capture an outsized return.







Figure 7: AI wage premiums were highest in low-adoption industries such as Food Services (~86%) and Health Care (~68%), while early-adopting sectors like Finance & Insurance showed compressed premiums (~24%), suggesting diminishing returns as adoption spreads.



7. Where Does AI Create the Most Value?
Combining the wage premium with the share of industry workers actually at AI firms paints a fuller picture. The upper-left quadrant (high premium, low workforce share) marks industries where AI is still a genuine differentiator. As adoption spreads rightward, the premium compresses. Bubble size reflects total employment.







Figure 8: Industries with low AI workforce share and high wage premiums (upper-left quadrant) represent sectors where AI remains a competitive differentiator. As adoption spreads, the premium compresses. Bubble size reflects total industry employment.



8. The AI Hubs of America
Where does AI adoption cluster geographically? California dominates, but this is largely a proxy for industrial composition. States with denser concentrations of Management, Information, and Professional Services firms show higher adoption rates. The state itself carries near-zero predictive power once firm characteristics are known.







Figure 9: California leads the U.S. in AI adoption rates, largely driven by its concentration of Management, Information, and Professional Services firms. Geographic variation in adoption reflects industrial composition more than state-level factors.



8.5: The Tech Hubs of America







Figure 10: Existing tech hubs within the U.S. Older, established tech hubs are in dark blue, with up-and-coming tech hubs in light blue.



9. Where are AI Companies Generating the Highest Revenue?
Pivoting back to revenue, are states across the US truly seeing success? As it turns out, the AI hubs found previously are also associated with the highest amount of total revenue. California in particular generates nearly 4 Trillion dollars in revenue, with Texas following behind. As for most of the other states, their revenue falls well below 1 Trillion dollars.







Figure 11: California and Texas led all states in total revenue in 2017, while most states generated well under $1 trillion, highlighting the geographic concentration of economic activity.



9.5: What Are the Highest Revenue-Generating Industries in Each State?
In terms of industry, the highest revenue generated type across each US state is by far Wholesale Trade, followed by Manufacturing in the Eastern US. Interestingly, New York displays their Finance and Insurance industry as the one with the most revenue, which makes sense given as Wall Street is located in Lower Manhattan.







Figure 12: Wholesale Trade dominated as the top revenue-generating industry across most U.S. states in 2017, with Manufacturing leading in parts of the Eastern U.S. and Finance & Insurance topping New York.

title: AI Usage in the U.S. Companies
text:
Conclusions
Artificial Intelligence has shifted from a productivity-enhancing tool to a potential threat to jobs—especially entry-level roles, as highlighted by Reuters recent study.
Our analysis captures the early stage of AI adoption. We find that early-adopting industries experienced stronger payroll growth compared to low adopters, along with differences in wage premiums across industries. We also identify the geographic concentration of AI activity, highlighting emerging tech hubs and their evolution across states.
To complement this, we use a Random Forest model to predict AI adoption. Results show that firm scale is the main driver, and adding pre-2018 variables significantly improves accuracy. Permutation importance suggests adoption reflects an underlying dimension of firm size rather than any single metric.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI Usage in the U.S. Companies
text:
Limitations and Future Work
Though we were able to conduct a relatively in-depth analysis of 2018 AI usage trends, our work is not without its limitations. Further research is still necessary to fully understand how AI was, and is currently, being used by firms across the U.S. and the reasons behind its implementation. Finally, this analysis is observational, so we cannot establish whether AI adoption causes payroll growth—this remains an open question for future research.

We identify three main limitations in our project:

Limited years of AI data availability. 

Limited AI usage & industry business characteristics. 

Lack of firm-level data.


Following these limitations, we recommend that subsequent research analyze multiple years of AI usage utilizing specific industry and firm-level data. Subsequent research may find it imperative to look into questions such as: 

How exactly AI was used in individual businesses (ex. spreadsheet creation, email responses)? 

How may AI usage vary across other firm/industry characteristics (ex. revenue, ownership structure, and geographic scope)? 

How has AI usage varied throughout the years? 


While our research serves as a fundamental first step in understanding AI usage trends, further studies will serve to capture a more complete view of our topic.

title: David’s Exploration
section: Who’s the top industry using AI?
text:
Who’s the top industry using AI?
I am interested in seeing who was the top industry in the “AI race” in 2018.

Codeai_industry_leaders <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence", 
    !is.na(used_tech), 
    used_tech != "Unknown") |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    ai_firms = sum(n_firms[used_tech == "Used"], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(ai_rate = ai_firms / total_firms) |>
  filter(total_firms > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(ai_rate))

ai_industry_leaders |> 
  filter(ai_firms>0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(naics_label, ai_rate), y = ai_rate, fill = ai_rate)) +
  geom_col() + 
  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette = "PuBu",
    direction = 1)+  coord_flip() + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Which Industries Led the AI Race in 2018?",
    subtitle = "Percentage of firms reporting AI Use",
    x = NULL,
    y = "AI Adoption Rate",
    caption = "Management took the lead in 2018, with an AI implementation of 7.7%. \nSource: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios ") +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), 
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "")

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Payroll Evolution
Level Change
I then got interested in analyzing what’s the payroll evolution by industry, but this was not telling me much so I also looked at the Payroll Percentage Change instead, and I took 2017 as the base year.

Codetech_2018_summary <- tech_2018_clean |> 
  group_by(year, used_tech, use_level) |> 
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop")

payroll_all <- cs_panel_clean |>
  group_by(year, naics_label) |>
  summarise(total_payroll = sum(payroll * 1000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

top_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |> slice_max(ai_rate, n = 4) |> pull(naics_label)
payroll_top4 <- payroll_all |> filter(naics_label %in% top_4_industries)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = payroll_all,  
            aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, group = naics_label), 
            color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(data = payroll_top4, 
            aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, color = naics_label, group = naics_label), 
            linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = payroll_top4, 
             aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, color = naics_label), 
             size = 1.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale = 1e-9, suffix = " B", accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "H") +
  labs(
    title = "Payroll Evolution",
    subtitle = "Highlighting the 4 industries with the highest AI adoption rates in 2018",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Annual Payroll (Billions)",
    color = NULL,
    caption = "Healthcare is the top industry by total payroll, despite showing low AI adoption rates in 2018.  \nSource: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios "
  ) + theme(legend.position = "top")







Percentage Change

Code## indexed payroll growth
payroll_growth <- payroll_all |>
  arrange(naics_label, year) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  mutate(
    payroll_growth_pct = total_payroll / first(total_payroll)) |> #base year: 2017
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(naics_label = str_wrap(naics_label, width = 35))


## Top AI industries
top_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |>
  slice_max(ai_rate, n = 4) |>
  pull(naics_label)

payroll_growth_top4 <- payroll_growth |>
  filter(naics_label %in% top_4_industries)


## Bottom AI industries
w_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |>
  slice_min(ai_rate, n = 4) |>
  pull(naics_label)

payroll_growth_w4 <- payroll_growth |>
  filter(naics_label %in% w_4_industries)


# plots
p1_top4_ai <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_growth,

 theme(

text:
Revenue
We only have revenue for the yeaer 2017, so I also looked at the revenue for that year by industry.






Note



FACT CHECK: The US Census Bureau also reported this exact figure as the one in my visualization.



Coderevenue_2017 <- cs_panel_clean |>

title: David’s Exploration
text:
The AI Wage Premium
The evidence suggests that AI-adopting firms consistently pay higher average wages per employee, but the gap shrinks as firms become larger, indicating diminishing differences in wage premia at scale.
Takeaways:

Smaller firms show a clearer wage advantage for AI adopters
Larger firms exhibit a weaker AI–wage gap
Overall pattern suggests heterogeneous returns to AI adoption across firm size


Code## wage premium story
tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
         used_tech != "Unknown",
         naics_sector != "Total", 
         payroll > 0,
         n_emp > 0) |>
  mutate(
    ai_used = if_else(used_tech == "Used", "AI Adopters", "Non-Adopters"),
    payroll_per_emp = payroll / n_emp) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = n_emp, y = payroll_per_emp, color = ai_used)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.5) +  # method = "mgcv",
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(suffix = "K"), 
    breaks = seq(0, 300, by = 50)) +  
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "E") +
  labs(
    title = "Does AI Pay?",
    subtitle = "Evidence of higher payroll standards among technology adopters across firm scales",
    x = "Number of Employees (Log Scale)",
    y = "Avg. Payroll per Employee (USD)",
    color = "Adoption Status",
    caption = "Payroll standardized to thousands of dollars per employee. Trend lines calculated via local regression (LOESS). \n Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8))

title: David’s Exploration
text:
AI Hubs in the US
Geographically, where is AI growing the most rapidly? Or where is it AI adoption rate higher?

Code# AI Adoption Rate by State
state_ai_adoption <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    !is.na(used_tech),
    used_tech != "Unknown") |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),

    state = str_pad(state, 2, pad = "0"),

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Wage Gaps and Premium
But is it worth to pay the cost? Are the revenues generated from AI higher than the cost of it?

Codetech_2018_summary <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    !is.na(used_tech),
    MOTUSETECH_LABEL == "All firms",
    IMPACTWF_U_LABEL == "All firms"    ) |>
  group_by(year, tech_type, used_tech, use_level) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    emp_per_firm = if_else(total_firms > 0, total_emp / total_firms, NA_real_),
    avg_wage     = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_))

industry_tech_summary <- tech_2018_clean |> 
  filter(
    !is.na(used_tech), 
    MOTUSETECH_LABEL == "All firms",   
    IMPACTWF_U_LABEL == "All firms"   ) |> 
  group_by(year, naics_label, tech_type, used_tech, use_level) |> 
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop") |> 
  mutate(
    emp_per_firm = if_else(total_firms > 0, total_emp / total_firms, NA_real_),
    avg_wage     = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_))

ai_industry_wages <- industry_tech_summary |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Did not use", "Used")) |>
  group_by(naics_label, used_tech) |>
  summarise(
    total_emp = sum(total_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(total_payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(avg_wage = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_)) |>
  filter(total_emp > 0) 

# one row per industry with both groups side-by-side
wage_wide <- ai_industry_wages |>
  filter(used_tech %in% c("Did not use", "Used")) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  filter(n() == 2) |>   # only industries where BOTH groups exist
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols    = naics_label,
    names_from = used_tech,
    values_from = c(avg_wage, total_emp, total_payroll)) |>
  rename(
    wage_no_ai = `avg_wage_Did not use`,
    wage_ai    = `avg_wage_Used`,
    emp_no_ai  = `total_emp_Did not use`,
    emp_ai     = `total_emp_Used`) 

 wage_wide <- wage_wide |>
  mutate(
    wage_gap = wage_ai - wage_no_ai,
    premium_pct = if_else(wage_no_ai > 0, wage_gap / wage_no_ai, NA_real_),
    total_emp = emp_no_ai + emp_ai,
    ai_emp_share = if_else(total_emp > 0, emp_ai / total_emp, NA_real_))



Code##  The AI Wage Gap by Industry
dumbbell_long <- wage_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(wage_no_ai, wage_ai),
    names_to = "group",
    values_to = "avg_wage") |>
  mutate(
    group = if_else(group == "wage_ai", "AI Adopters", "Non-Adopters"),
    naics_label = reorder(naics_label, wage_gap))

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = wage_wide |> mutate(naics_label = reorder(naics_label, wage_gap)),
    aes(x = wage_no_ai, xend = wage_ai,
        y = naics_label, yend = naics_label),
    color = "grey75", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(
    data = dumbbell_long,
    aes(x = avg_wage, y = naics_label, color = group),
    size = 4) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar(suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "The AI Wage Gap by Industry",
    subtitle = "AI adopters vs. non-adopters (2018)",
    x = "Avg. Annual Payroll per Employee (Thousands USD)",
    y = NULL,
    color = NULL,
    caption = "Source: 2018 ABS") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8))






Code## AI Wage Premium: Who Benefits Most?

wage_wide |>
  mutate(
    naics_label  = reorder(naics_label, premium_pct),
    premium_sign = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, "Positive", "Negative")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = premium_pct, y = naics_label, fill = premium_sign)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.5) +
  # Label the bars with the % value
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = scales::percent(premium_pct, accuracy = 1),
      hjust = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, -0.15, 1.15)
    ),
    size = 3, color = "grey20"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.08, 0.10))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2d6a4f", "Negative" = "#c1121f")) +
  labs(
    title    = "AI Wage Premium: Who Benefits Most?",
    subtitle = "% difference in avg. pay between AI and non-AI firms, by industry (2018)",
    x        = "Wage Premium (%)",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)\nNote: Food Services and Information sectors show the largest wage premiums for AI-adopting firms."
  )






Code## Employment Scale vs. Wage Premium
ggplot(
  wage_wide |> mutate(premium_sign = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, "Positive", "Negative")),
  aes(
    x = ai_emp_share,
    y = premium_pct,
    size = total_emp,
    color = premium_sign,
    label = naics_label)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.15, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.65) +
  geom_text(
    size = 3,
    color = "grey20") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05)) ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(-0.15, 0.75) ) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(3, 16),
    labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M", accuracy = 1),
    name = "Total Employees" ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Positive" = "#2d6a4f", "Negative" = "#c1121f"),
    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Where Does AI Create the Most Value?",
    subtitle = "Share of industry workforce at AI firms vs. wage premium",
    x = "Share of Industry Employees at AI Firms",
    y = "AI Wage Premium",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",

    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Takeaways

 AI adoption in 2018 was driven by firm scale, not industry wealth or geography.**


Adoption did not follow the money

Management led AI adoption at 7.7% (Figure 1), yet ranked near the bottom in total 2017 revenue (Figure 4). Wholesale Trade and Manufacturing, the two largest industries by revenue, were mid-tier adopters at best.






Note



Knowledge-intensive sectors moved first because AI fits their work, not because they had the deepest pockets.



Geography is a proxy for industrial composition

California dominates the adoption map, but the random forest assigns State near-zero permutation importance once firm characteristics are known. The geographic pattern reflects where Management, Information, and Professional Services firms concentrate — not something intrinsic to location.

Payroll growth favoured AI-adopting industries — with a COVID caveat

The top four AI adopters grew payroll steadily from 2017 to 2021. Lower-adoption sectors, particularly Agriculture and Arts & Recreation, showed sharp swings around 2019–2020.

The forest confirmed scale as the dominant factor

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Machine Learning (See David’s Appendix)

The single decision tree splits first on number of firms ≥ 558, then on employees per firm ≥ 61, a profile that maps directly onto the concentrated, knowledge-intensive industries. Adding 2017 panel variables (revenue per firm and average industry wage) cut OOB prediction error from 5.3% to 3.1%, the largest accuracy gain in the analysis.
The Gini vs. permutation comparison shows that Gini inflated the apparent importance of continuous size variables. Permutation importance redistributed credit more evenly and elevated employees-per-firm, suggesting AI adoption reflects a single latent dimension of firm scale rather than any one metric in isolation.
This analysis is observational. Whether AI adoption caused the subsequent payroll divergence, or whether the same organizational characteristics that predict adoption also predict payroll growth, cannot be resolved with this data, and remains the natural next step.

title: Isauro’s EDA
text:
Spatial Viz: Revenue

Code# revenue has only been reported for 2017, cleaning is necessary
revenue_2017 <- cs_panel_clean |>
  filter(year == 2017)


Revenue per State

Code# spatial visualization (total revenue)
state_revenue_2017 <- revenue_2017 |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue/1000000000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

rev_map <- us_map |>
  left_join(state_revenue_2017, by = c("GEOID" = "state"))

spatialplotCont(rev_map, total_revenue) +
  labs(
    title = "Where are AI Companies Generating the Highest Revenue?",
    subtitle = "Total State Revenue in 2017",
    fill = "Total Revenue \n (in Trillions$)",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey")








Code# spatial visualization (revenue per firm)
state_revenue_2017_per_firm <- revenue_2017 |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(avg_revenue = sum(revenue_per_firm/1000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

rev_per_firm_map <- us_map |>
  left_join(state_revenue_2017_per_firm, by = c("GEOID" = "state"))

spatialplotCont(rev_per_firm_map, avg_revenue) +
  labs(
    title = "Where are AI Companies Generating the Highest Revenue?",
    subtitle = "State Revenue per firm in 2017",
    fill = "Total Revenue \n (in Millions$)",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey")







When looking at the maps for revenue across each states, the results vary drastically when observing it on a total revenue scale, and a revenue per firm scale. For the former, California proves to be the highest profitable AI state, followed by Texas by a difference of nearly $1 Trillion. As for the latter scale, the revenue is a lot more evenly distributed across each state, with the East Coast generating more revenue per firm compared to other regions in the US.
Highest Profitable Industry in Each State

  slice_max(total_revenue, n = 1)

Observing the two maps based on the same Total Revenue and Revenue per Firms scales, they paint very distinct pictures from one another. The first map shows that Wholesale Trade has the generate the highest amount of revenue for the vast majority of states, with Manufacturing following second. The second map tells a different story, Utilities is the most profitable industry (on a revenue per firm scale) for the vast majority of the country, with Louisiana and Mississippi differing by having Manufacturing, and Hawaii having Finance and Insurance.

title: Katherine’s Exploration
text:
Motivations
In this document, I primarily look at disaggregated AI usage, both across states and industries. I exclude total and unknown values to focus on known data. Alongside my teammates, I only focus on observations from 2018 as this was the only year with data on AI usage.
I also look at current (May 2026) job trends related to AI, to determine whether current trends are similar to what we see in our data.

With AI usage soaring in recent years, our way of life has drastically changed. For this project, we will observe how this surge has impacted businesses across the US.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Introduction
Source: THE DIFFERENCES IN HOW CNN, MSNBC & FOX COVER THE NEWS pudding.coo
In this document, we will be talking about the data story structure of the article “The differences in how CNN, MSNBC & Fox Cover News.” This is accessed through The Pudding’s website, which we believe is a competition where people can narrate with the data. The author of this article is Charlie Smart.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What Did They Do?

Clustering technique, but simple visualizations.

Visualization animations based on user scrolling bring the reader’s attention to different, important aspects of the visualizations and different subsets in the data. Readers can hover the cursor over individual points.

Building the visualization with the reader.

Each visualization starts off bare. With each paragraph of text to the side of the viz, the author adds more data to the visualization, allowing text and viz to complement each other.   

Search bars & filtering options for specific visualizations. 

After the visualizations have been explored fully, the author gives the user a search bar, in which the user can input their own topics/key words and see how they’re reflected in each plot.
Audiences can also filter data points/subsets of the data, allowing them to focus on aspects of the dataset that they are interested in. 

Interactive text. 

Certain words (ex. Month names, definitions) in the text allow audiences to hover their cursor over it, which either results in the respective portion of a graph being highlighted or visualizations of the definition being described to pop up on screen. This allows audiences to achieve a more complete understanding of what the author is talking about.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Implications & Methodology

What’s the purpose of these plots?

Following the visualizations, the author makes light of the scope of political polarization, warning the user to be aware of the news they are receiving and the agenda they may be pushing. This caution makes the visualizations even stronger, as they highlight this warning in an extremely effective way.

What was the author’s creation process?

At the end of the article, the author adds a section on Data and Methodology, allowing audiences better insight into data sources/what choices went into data collection, and other underlying data aspects that would otherwise be hidden in the visualizations.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
CONCLUSIONS

The audience can draw their own conclusions based on which words are used most by the big 3 news outlets: CNN, MSNBC, and FOX. This is informative as we can see which ones are biased to certain positions or ideas. This is precisely what the authors of this project try to do by selecting keywords the audience might be interested in or events happening during that time, such as the Russian war, hurricanes, Trump, Hillary, etc. 
Most importantly, we gathered more ideas on the possible direction our project can take. As our project is on the AI implementation in U.S. businesses, we probably won’t do it based on words, but rather in companies, highlighting the outliers but also those left behind. What industries are not catching up in the AI implementation? What companies are close to the 100% AI implementation? Is it possible to get 100% implementation?

title: Appendix C — David’s Appendix
text:
Predicting AI Usage with ML
After doing my EDA in the previous document, I was interested in whether we can predict which firms adopt AI. Thus, I decided to run a Random Forest.
Random Forest
The random forest algorithm extends bagging by combining both bagging and feature randomness to create an uncorrelated forest of decision trees. Decision trees work by asking a series of questions that split the data into branches. They are trained using the Classification and Regression Tree (CART) algorithm, with metrics like Gini impurity, information gain, or MSE (mean std. errors) used to evaluate split quality.
Building the Forest
While initial results using Gini-based importance suggested that firm size variables overwhelmingly drive AI adoption, this measure is known to be biased toward continuous predictors. Using permutation importance — which evaluates the impact of each variable on model accuracy — provides a more reliable ranking. The results confirm that firm scale remains the primary predictor of AI adoption, followed by workforce intensity and industry sector. In contrast, geographic location (state) contributes no predictive power once firm characteristics are considered.
Because firm size variables are highly correlated, permutation importance likely distributes predictive contribution across them, suggesting that AI adoption is driven by an underlying latent factor of firm scale rather than any single metric.

Codetidymodels_prefer()

# 2017 industry financials (pre-adoption context)
industry_financials <- cs_panel_clean |>
  filter(year == 2017) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(
    revenue_per_firm = mean(revenue_per_firm, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_wage_panel   = mean(avg_wage,         na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")

#  9-predictor dataset
rf_data_9 <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Used", "Did not use"),
    !is.na(n_emp), !is.na(payroll), !is.na(n_firms),
    n_emp > 0, payroll > 0, n_firms > 0,
    naics_sector != "Total" ) |>
  mutate(
    ai_adopted   = as.factor(if_else(used_tech == "Used", "Yes", "No")),
    avg_wage     = payroll / n_emp,
    emp_per_firm = n_emp / n_firms,
    naics_label  = as.factor(naics_label),
    state        = as.factor(state) ) |>
  left_join(industry_financials, by = "naics_label") |>
  select(
    ai_adopted,
    # 7-predictor core
    n_emp, n_firms, avg_wage, emp_per_firm, payroll, naics_label, state,
    # +2 from panel
    revenue_per_firm, avg_wage_panel ) |>
  drop_na()

# 7-predictor subset
rf_data_7 <- rf_data_9 |>
  select(-revenue_per_firm, -avg_wage_panel)

Code# settings: 1000 trees, min_n = 2, sqrt(p) predictors tried per split -- AKA random forest
make_spec <- function(importance_type) {
  rand_forest() |>
    set_mode("classification") |>
    set_engine("ranger") |>
    set_args(
      mtry        = NULL,       
      trees       = 1000,
      min_n       = 2,
      probability = FALSE,
      importance  = importance_type)
}

spec_gini  <- make_spec("impurity")    # Gini / mean decrease in impurity
spec_perm  <- make_spec("permutation") # Permutation importance


# Fit all four forests
set.seed(212)
forest_7_gini <- spec_gini |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_7)
forest_7_perm <- spec_perm |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_7)

set.seed(212)
forest_9_gini <- spec_gini |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_9)
forest_9_perm <- spec_perm |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_9)

Code# Importance extraction helper
get_importance <- function(forest, method_label, n_pred_label) {
  forest |>
    extract_fit_engine() |>
    pluck("variable.importance") |>
    enframe(name = "Variable", value = "Importance") |>
    mutate(
      Variable   = recode(Variable, !!!var_labels),
      Method     = method_label,
      Predictors = n_pred_label,
      # Normalise within each forest so Gini and Permutation scales are comparable
      Importance = Importance / max(abs(Importance)))
}

importance_all <- bind_rows(
  get_importance(forest_7_gini, "Gini",        "7 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_7_perm, "Permutation",  "7 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_9_gini, "Gini",        "9 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_9_perm, "Permutation",  "9 predictors"))

# Importance comparison helper
plot_importance_comparison <- function(data, title_suffix) {
  data |>
    select(Variable, Method, Importance) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = Method, values_from = Importance) |>
    mutate(Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, Gini)) |>
    ggplot(aes(y = Variable)) +
    geom_segment(
      aes(x = Permutation, xend = Gini, yend = Variable),
      color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Gini,        color = "Gini"),        size = 3) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Permutation, color = "Permutation"), size = 3) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "E", begin = 0.2, end = 0.8) + # ← changed
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    labs(
      title    = glue::glue("Gini vs Permutation Importance — {title_suffix}"),
      subtitle = "Normalised 0–1 within each method. Dots far apart = Gini bias.",
      x = "Normalised importance", y = NULL, color = NULL,
      caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}

#  importance plot
importance_all |>
  select(Variable, Method, Importance, Predictors) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Method, values_from = Importance) |>
  mutate(
    x_start  = ifelse(Gini > Permutation, Gini - 0.02, Gini + 0.02),
    x_end    = ifelse(Gini > Permutation, Permutation + 0.02, Permutation - 0.02),
    Variable = paste0(Variable, "___", Predictors),
    Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, Permutation)) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = Variable)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = x_start, xend = x_end, yend = Variable),
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.18, "cm"), type = "closed", ends = "last"),
    color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(aes(x = Gini,        color = "Gini"),        size = 2.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x = Permutation, color = "Permutation"), size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "E", begin = 0.2, end = 0.8) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = ~ sub("___.*", "", .x)) +      # ← strip the key suffix
  facet_wrap(~ Predictors, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),

  labs(title    = "Variable Importance: All Four Models",
    subtitle = "Arrow points Gini → Permutation; longer arrow = greater Gini overestimation",

    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey + 2017 Economic Census")+

  set_args(cost_complexity = 0.02, tree_depth = 4) |>

    data = rf_data_7)   # no state & naics_label to keep the tree readable
